intro: Transitioning from C to C++ steps into a powerful multi-paradigm programming layer while preserving direct hardware execution and zero-overhead performance. While maintaining backward compatibility with standard C layouts, C++ introduces clean object-oriented encapsulation, strong type checks, and type-safe templates. Developers make this migration to introduce better structural design patterns and eliminate unsafe type casts without losing execution speed.

diffs.from: Statically typed, weakly enforced procedural variables allowing unrestricted implicit pointer conversions.
diffs.to: Statically typed, nominal system with strict type checking, function overloading, and compile-time templates.

diffs.from: Procedural syntax based entirely on functions, flat structure definitions, and explicit manual configuration hooks.
diffs.to: Multi-paradigm syntax supporting classes, namespaces, operator definitions, and clean reference modifiers (&).

diffs.from: Manually controlled via explicit heap allocations (malloc) and manual cleanup code execution (free).
diffs.to: Resource acquisition is initialization (RAII) semantics, stack destruction hooks, smart pointers, and new/delete operators.

diffs.from: No built-in language abstractions, depending entirely on host platform threading frameworks (such as pthreads).
diffs.to: Built-in concurrency support (std::thread), async task futures, atomic variables, and language-level synchronization blocks.

diffs.from: Lean library managing fundamental console streams, file pointers, math algorithms, and raw memory allocations.
diffs.to: Standard Template Library (STL) delivering highly optimized generic collections, stream-based I/O pipelines, and search algorithms.

examples.title: Encapsulated Object Classes
examples.description: Refactoring separate procedural C structs and function chains into an encapsulated, class-based C++ object layout.

examples.title: Type-Safe Dynamic Allocations
examples.description: Upgrading manual byte-calculated C dynamic allocations into type-safe C++ constructors and smart pointers.

tips.title: Prefer References over Pointers
tips.body: Use reference parameters (Type& arg) instead of raw pointers (Type* arg) to guarantee non-null arguments and clean up method call syntax.

tips.title: Adopt Smart Heap Containers
tips.body: Stop calling raw malloc() and free() operations manually. Use std::unique_ptr or std::shared_ptr to handle dynamic object lifecycles safely.

tips.title: Swap Arrays for std::vector
tips.body: Replace primitive pointer arrays with type-safe std::vector containers to let the library manage dynamic element resizing securely.

tips.title: Encapsulate Global Constants
tips.body: Replace legacy preprocessor macros (#define) with type-safe 'constexpr' declarations and scope-controlled namespaces.
